title: A Christmas Carol1
content:
آ آ آ آ Three damsels in the queenï؟½s chamber,
آ آ آ آ The queenï؟½s mouth was most fair;
آ آ آ آ She spake a word of Godï؟½s mother
آ آ آ آ As the combs went in her hair.
آ آ آ آ Mary that is of might,
آ آ آ آ Bring us to thy Sonï؟½s sight.

آ آ آ آ They held the gold combs out from her,
آ آ آ آ A spanï؟½s length off her head;
آ آ آ آ She sang this song of Godï؟½s mother
آ آ آ آ And of her bearing-bed.
آ آ آ آ Mary most full of grace,
آ آ آ آ Bring us to thy Sonï؟½s face.

آ آ آ آ When she sat at Josephï؟½s hand,
آ آ آ آ She looked against her side;
آ آ آ آ And either way from the short silk band
آ آ آ آ Her girdle was all wried.
آ آ آ آ Mary that all good may,
آ آ آ آ Bring us to thy Sonï؟½s way.

آ آ آ آ Mary had three women for her bed,
آ آ آ آ The twain were maidens clean;
آ آ آ آ The first of them had white and red,
آ آ آ آ The third had riven green.
آ آ آ آ Mary that is so sweet,
آ آ آ آ Bring us to thy Sonï؟½s feet.

آ آ آ آ She had three women for her hair,
آ آ آ آ Two were gloved soft and shod;
آ آ آ آ The third had feet and fingers bare,
آ آ آ آ She was the likest God.
آ آ آ آ Mary that wieldeth land,
آ آ آ آ Bring us to thy Sonï؟½s hand.

آ آ آ آ She had three women for her ease,
آ آ آ آ The twain were good women:
آ آ آ آ The first two were the two Maries,
آ آ آ آ The third was Magdalen.
آ آ آ آ Mary that perfect is,
آ آ آ آ Bring us to thy Sonï؟½s kiss.

آ آ آ آ Joseph had three workmen in his stall,
آ آ آ آ To serve him well upon;
آ آ آ آ The first of them were Peter and Paul,
آ آ آ آ The third of them was John.
آ آ آ آ Mary, Godï؟½s handmaiden,
آ آ آ آ Bring us to thy Sonï؟½s ken.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½If your child be none other manï؟½s,
آ آ آ آ But if it be very mine,
آ آ آ آ The bedstead shall be gold two spans,
آ آ آ آ The bedfoot silver fine.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Mary that made God mirth,
آ آ آ آ Bring us to thy Sonï؟½s birth.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½If the child be some other manï؟½s,
آ آ آ آ And if it be none of mine,
آ آ آ آ The manger shall be straw two spans,
آ آ آ آ Betwixen kine and kine.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Mary that made sin cease,
آ آ آ آ Bring us to thy Sonï؟½s peace.

آ آ آ آ Christ was born upon this wise,
آ آ آ آ It fell on such a night,
آ آ آ آ Neither with sounds of psalteries,
آ آ آ آ Nor with fire for light.
آ آ آ آ Mary that is Godï؟½s spouse,
آ آ آ آ Bring us to thy Sonï؟½s house.

آ آ آ آ The star came out upon the east
آ آ آ آ With a great sound and sweet:
آ آ آ آ Kings gave gold to make him feast
آ آ آ آ And myrrh for him to eat.
آ آ آ آ Mary, of thy sweet mood,
آ آ آ آ Bring us to thy Sonï؟½s good.

آ آ آ آ He had two handmaids at his head,
آ آ آ آ One handmaid at his feet;
آ آ آ آ The twain of them were fair and red,
آ آ آ آ The third one was right sweet.
آ آ آ آ Mary that is most wise,
آ آ آ آ Bring us to thy Sonï؟½s eyes. Amen.
meaning: About: Algernon Charles Swinburne (April 5, 1837 ï؟½ April 10, 1909) was a Victorian era English poet. His poetry was highly controversial in its day, much of it containing recurring themes of sadomasochism, death-wish, lesbianism and irreligion.
